narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 27, full-time student status, and low year-end deciles in school rankings—and calculated a 96% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate without issues. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also in the lowest 10% compared to the average around the middle, reinforcing the model's concern about your early academic trajectory. Your LSAT score of 27, well below the dataset average of about 37, further tipped the scales toward failure, as lower scores like yours consistently correlate with bar struggles in the model's analysis. While factors like your full-time enrollment aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%), they weren't enough to offset these academic red flags. In comparison to typical applicants who pass the bar, your grades and test scores stand out as significantly weaker, placing you in a high-risk group the model has learned to flag. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 35 or higher, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings in future academic settings. Building a stronger academic foundation could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your next application could tell a different story with focused improvements.
